ladders.title: Full Problem — Word Dictionary with Wildcard Search using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Implement a word dictionary supporting addWord and search operations with '.' wildcard matching using brute force list iteration.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete problem solution using the brute force approach. We maintain a list to store all added words. For search operations, we iterate through every word in the list and check if it matches the search pattern, handling '.' wildcards by character-by-character comparison. The intuition is straightforward: since we have no indexing structure, we must examine every word to find matches. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty list. 2) addWord(word): append word to list. 3) search(pattern): iterate through each stored word; for each word, check if length equals pattern length; if yes, compare characters one by one, treating '.' as matching any character; return True if any word matches; else return False. Time complexity: O(n*m) for search where n is number of words, m is average word length; O(1) for addWord. Space: O(n*m) to store all words. Connection to L1: This uses the pattern matching subroutine from L1 repeatedly. Common mistakes: forgetting to check length equality first (optimization), not handling empty patterns correctly, inefficient nested loops. Real code pattern: class WordDictionary: def __init__(self): self.words = []; def addWord(self, word): self.words.append(word); def search(self, pattern): for word in self.words: if len(word) != len(pattern): continue; match = True; for i in range(len(pattern)): if pattern[i] != '.' and pattern[i] != word[i]: match = False; break; if match: return True; return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Pattern Matching with Wildcards
ladders.desc: Check if a single word matches a search pattern containing '.' wildcards.
ladders.explanation: This is the core matching function used repeatedly in the brute force solution. Given a word and a pattern (both strings), determine if they match character by character, where '.' in the pattern matches any character in the word. The intuition: since wildcards can match any single character, we can compare positions sequentially, only failing when we encounter a non-wildcard mismatch. The algorithm: 1) If lengths differ, return False immediately (optimization). 2) Iterate i from 0 to len(pattern)-1: if pattern[i] != '.' and pattern[i] != word[i], return False. 3) If loop completes, return True. Time: O(m) where m is word length. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each word in the list during search. Common mistakes: not checking length first (wastes time), off-by-one errors in loop bounds, misunderstanding '.' as matching zero characters (it matches exactly one). Real code pattern: def matches(word, pattern): if len(word) != len(pattern): return False; for i in range(len(pattern)): if pattern[i] != '.' and pattern[i] != word[i]: return False; return True.

Length check: both length 3. i=0: 'd' vs 'd' match. i=1: '.' matches 'o'. i=2: 'g' vs 'g' match. Return True.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative List Search with Conditional Matching
ladders.desc: Search through a list of words to find any that match a pattern.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the iterative search logic without the full dictionary implementation. Given a list of words and a pattern, return True if any word matches the pattern using character comparison with wildcards. The intuition: since we have no indexing, we must check each word sequentially until a match is found or list is exhausted. The algorithm: 1) For each word in list: if lengths equal, compare characters as in L1; if match found, return True immediately. 2) If no matches after loop, return False. Time: O(n*m) where n is list size, m is average length. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: Uses the matches function from L1. Common mistakes: not breaking early on match (inefficient), incorrect handling of empty lists. Real code pattern: def search_list(words, pattern): for word in words: if len(word) == len(pattern): match = True; for i in range(len(pattern)): if pattern[i] != '.' and pattern[i] != word[i]: match = False; break; if match: return True; return False.

The intuition: since lengths are equal, we can compare each corresponding character; a mismatch occurs only when both are non-wildcard and different. The algorithm: 1) Assume lengths are equal (caller ensures). 2) For i in range(length): if str1[i] != '.' and str2[i] != '.' and str1[i] != str2[i], return False; but actually pattern has '.' while word does not. approaches.name: Trie with DFS O(26^d)
approaches.summary: Build a Trie from all words and perform a depth-first search (DFS) during querying to explore all possible paths when encountering a '.' wildcard. This leverages the Trie structure to efficiently prune searches but can be slow for many wildcards.

approaches.spaceComplexity: O(t) where t is total characters in Trie

ladders.title: Full Problem — Word Dictionary with Wildcard Search using Trie and DFS
ladders.desc: Implement a word dictionary supporting insertion and search with '.' wildcard using Trie with DFS exploration.
ladders.explanation: This solution implements a complete word dictionary that supports two operations: adding words to the dictionary and searching for words that may contain '.' wildcards. The core data structure is a Trie (prefix tree) where each node contains an array of child pointers (one for each lowercase letter) and a boolean flag indicating if a word ends at that node. For insertion, we traverse the Trie character by character, creating nodes as needed, and mark the end node. For search, when we encounter a '.' wildcard, we perform DFS to explore all possible child paths (26 possibilities for lowercase letters). The Trie structure helps prune searches when paths don't exist, but worst-case complexity occurs when we have many wildcards. Intuition: The Trie organizes words by prefixes, allowing efficient prefix matching. When we hit a wildcard, we need to check all possible continuations, which DFS handles naturally by exploring each branch. The algorithm: 1. Define TrieNode class with children array[26] and is_end flag. 2. Insert: For each char in word, calculate index = ord(char)-97, create child if null, move to child, mark is_end at last node. 3. Search: Recursive DFS function that takes current node and current position in search word. Base cases: if position == len(word), return node.is_end. If current char is '.': iterate through all 26 children, if child exists, recursively search from child with position+1, return true if any path returns true. Else: calculate index for current char, if child exists, recursively search from child with position+1. Time: Insert O(L) per word where L is word length. Search worst-case O(26^d) where d is number of '.' wildcards, but typically faster due to Trie pruning. Space: O(T) where T is total characters in all words. Connection: This combines all lower-level concepts into a complete solution. Common mistakes: Not handling empty search strings correctly, forgetting to mark word endings, not pruning DFS when child is null. Real code pattern: class WordDictionary: def __init__(self): self.root = TrieNode(); def addWord(self, word): node = self.root; for ch in word: idx = ord(ch)-97; if not node.children[idx]: node.children[idx] = TrieNode(); node = node.children[idx]; node.is_end = True; def search(self, word): def dfs(node, pos): if pos == len(word): return node.is_end; ch = word[pos]; if ch == '.': for child in node.children: if child and dfs(child, pos+1): return True; return False; else: idx = ord(ch)-97; return node.children[idx] and dfs(node.children[idx], pos+1); return dfs(self.root, 0).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Search with Wildcard Handling in Trie
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive DFS function that searches for a word with wildcards in a Trie.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the search operation. It performs a depth-first search through the Trie to find if any word matches the given search string, which may contain '.' wildcards. The function takes a Trie node and a current position in the search string as parameters. Intuition: We traverse the Trie simultaneously with the search string. When we encounter a regular character, we follow the corresponding child if it exists. When we encounter a '.', we need to explore all possible children because the '.' can match any character. DFS allows us to systematically check each possibility, returning true as soon as we find a matching path. The Trie structure prunes invalid paths early when children are null. Algorithm: Define function dfs(node, pos): if pos equals length of search word: return node.is_end. Let ch = search_word[pos]. If ch == '.': for each child in node.children: if child is not null: if dfs(child, pos+1) returns true: return true. return false. Else: idx = ord(ch)-97. if node.children[idx] is null: return false. return dfs(node.children[idx], pos+1). Time: O(26^d) worst-case where d is number of '.' wildcards, but optimized by Trie pruning. Space: O(d) for recursion stack. Connection: This function is called by the main search method with the root node and position 0. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check node.is_end at the end, not handling null children correctly, infinite recursion if base case missing. Real code pattern: def dfs(node, pos): if pos == len(word): return node.is_end; ch = word[pos]; if ch == '.': for child in node.children: if child and dfs(child, pos+1): return True; return False; else: idx = ord(ch)-97; return node.children[idx] and dfs(node.children[idx], pos+1).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Handling Wildcard '.' in Trie Traversal
ladders.desc: Implement the logic to traverse all possible paths when encountering a '.' wildcard during Trie search.
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on how to handle the '.' wildcard character during search. When searching a Trie, normally we follow a specific child based on the current character. However, '.' matches any character, so we must consider all 26 possible lowercase letters. Intuition: At a Trie node, if the search character is '.', we cannot choose a single child; instead, we need to explore every existing child branch because any of them could lead to a matching word. This is essentially a branching operation where we fan out to multiple search paths. We use iteration over all children to implement this. Algorithm: At current node with search character ch: if ch == '.': for i from 0 to 25: if node.children[i] is not null: recursively search from node.children[i] with next position. If any recursive call returns true, return true. If all return false, return false. Time: O(26) per '.' in worst case, but often less due to null children. Space: O(1) for iteration, but recursion stack depends on depth. Connection: This logic is embedded within the DFS search function. Common mistakes: Not iterating over all 26 children, not checking for null children before recursing, not returning false after exhausting all options. Real code pattern: if ch == '.': for child in node.children: if child: if dfs(child, pos+1): return True; return False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Trie Node Structure and Insertion
ladders.desc: Implement the Trie node structure and the insertion of words into the Trie.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating the Trie data structure and adding words to it. A Trie node typically contains an array of pointers to child nodes (one for each lowercase letter) and a boolean flag to mark the end of a word. Intuition: The Trie organizes words by their prefixes, allowing efficient prefix-based operations. Each node represents a character, and the path from root to a node spells a prefix. Insertion builds this structure by adding nodes for each character in the word. Algorithm: Define TrieNode class with attributes: children array of size 26 initialized to null, and is_end boolean initialized to false. Insert function: Start at root node. For each character ch in word: calculate index = ord(ch)-97. If current node's children[index] is null, create a new TrieNode and assign to children[index]. Move current node to children[index]. After processing all characters, set current node.is_end = true. Time: O(L) per insertion where L is word length. Space: O(L) for new nodes if they don't exist. Connection: This provides the Trie structure that the search operation uses. Common mistakes: Not initializing children array properly, forgetting to mark word endings, incorrect index calculation. Real code pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = [None]*26; self.is_end = False. def insert(root, word): node = root; for ch in word: idx = ord(ch)-97; if not node.children[idx]: node.children[idx] = TrieNode(); node = node.children[idx]; node.is_end = True.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character to Index Mapping for Trie Children
ladders.desc: Convert a lowercase character to an array index (0-25) for accessing Trie children.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is fundamental to Trie implementation. Since Tries for lowercase English letters typically use an array of size 26 to store child pointers, we need a way to map each character to its corresponding index in this array. Intuition: Each character 'a' to 'z' has a unique ASCII code. By subtracting the ASCII code of 'a' from the character's code, we get an index from 0 to 25. This provides constant-time access to the child node for that character. Algorithm: For a given character ch, ensure it is lowercase (or convert if needed). Compute index = ord(ch) - ord('a'). Since ord('a') is 97, this simplifies to index = ord(ch) - 97. Use this index to access the children array: node.children[index]. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection: This mapping is used in both insertion (to find/create child nodes) and search (to traverse specific paths). Common mistakes: Using incorrect ASCII values, not handling non-lowercase characters, off-by-one errors. Real code pattern: idx = ord(ch) - 97; or idx = ord(ch) - ord('a');

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Trie Data Structure Basics
ladders.desc: Understand the Trie data structure: a tree used to store strings for efficient prefix searches.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves using an array to store references to child nodes in a tree node. In computer science, a tree node can have multiple children. For a Trie dealing with lowercase letters, we often use a fixed-size array of 26 elements, where each element is a pointer (or reference) to a child node corresponding to a letter from 'a' to 'z'. Intuition: The array provides direct access to child nodes via indices. If we know the character, we can compute its index and immediately access the child, enabling O(1) time for navigation. This is efficient compared to using a dictionary or linked list when the alphabet size is fixed and small. Algorithm: Define a class Node with an attribute children that is an array of size 26 initialized to null. Each position i in the array corresponds to character chr(97+i). To access child for character ch: index = ord(ch)-97; child = children[index]. To set child: children[index] = new_node. Time: O(1) for access. Space: O(26) per node. Connection: This array structure is used in TrieNode to store children. Common mistakes: Not initializing array to null, accessing out-of-bounds indices, misunderstanding pointer vs value. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self): self.children = [None] * 26.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Word Dictionary with Wildcard Search using Trie and BFS
ladders.desc: Implement a word dictionary supporting insert and search operations with '.' wildcards using Trie and BFS.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires building a data structure that stores words and supports searching with '.' wildcards that match any character. The solution uses a Trie (prefix tree) to store words efficiently, and BFS (breadth-first search) to handle wildcard searches. The intuition is that a Trie organizes words by their prefixes, making prefix searches efficient. For wildcard searches, when encountering a '.', we need to explore all possible character branches at that position. BFS allows us to process nodes level by level, which can be more intuitive than DFS for certain search patterns. The algorithm: 1. Define a TrieNode class with children array (size 26 for lowercase letters) and is_end flag. 2. Insert: Traverse the word character by character, creating nodes as needed, mark the end node. 3. Search: Use BFS with a queue. Start with the root node. For each character in the search word: if it's a letter, move to the corresponding child; if it's '.', enqueue all non-null children. If at any step no nodes remain in the queue, return false. After processing all characters, check if any node in the queue has is_end true. Time complexity: O(26^d) worst-case for d wildcards, as each '.' expands to up to 26 branches. Space: O(t + w) where t is Trie size and w is max queue size during BFS. Connection to L1: This builds on the BFS search subroutine. Common mistakes: Not handling empty search strings correctly, forgetting to reset the queue for each character, or not checking is_end at the end. Real code pattern: class WordDictionary: def __init__(self): self.root = TrieNode(); def addWord(self, word): node = self.root; for ch in word: idx = ord(ch)-97; if not node.children[idx]: node.children[idx] = TrieNode(); node = node.children[idx]; node.is_end = True; def search(self, word): queue = [self.root]; for ch in word: next_queue = []; for node in queue: if ch == '.': for child in node.children: if child: next_queue.append(child); else: idx = ord(ch)-97; if node.children[idx]: next_queue.append(node.children[idx]); queue = next_queue; if not queue: return False; return any(node.is_end for node in queue).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Search for Wildcard Pattern in Trie
ladders.desc: Implement the BFS search function that handles '.' wildcards in a Trie.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the search operation in the full problem. It takes a root Trie node and a search word (which may contain '.' wildcards) and returns whether the word exists in the Trie. The intuition is to use BFS to explore all possible paths simultaneously when encountering wildcards. BFS processes nodes level by level, which aligns with checking characters position by position. For each character in the search word, we maintain a queue of current Trie nodes. If the character is a letter, we filter the queue to nodes that have the corresponding child. If it's a '.', we expand the queue to include all children of current nodes. After processing all characters, we check if any node in the queue marks the end of a word. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize queue with root node. 2. For each character ch in search word: a. Initialize next_queue as empty. b. For each node in queue: if ch is '.', iterate through all children (0-25), add non-null children to next_queue; else, compute index idx = ord(ch)-97, if node.children[idx] exists, add it to next_queue. c. Set queue = next_queue. If queue becomes empty, return false early. 3. After loop, return true if any node in queue has is_end true, else false. Time complexity: O(26^d * L) where d is number of '.' and L is length, but typically O(N) for no wildcards. Space: O(w) where w is max queue size. Connection to L0: This function is called in the search method of WordDictionary. Common mistakes: Not handling empty search string (should check root.is_end? but root doesn't store words, so search '' should return false unless we insert empty string, but problem doesn't specify; assume false). Real code pattern: def bfs_search(root, word): queue = [root]; for ch in word: next_queue = []; for node in queue: if ch == '.': for child in node.children: if child: next_queue.append(child); else: idx = ord(ch)-97; if node.children[idx]: next_queue.append(node.children[idx]); queue = next_queue; if not queue: return False; return any(node.is_end for node in queue).

Process 't': idx=19, a-node.children[19]=t-node, next_queue=[t-node], queue=[t-node]. Check t-node.is_end=true, return true.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Handling Wildcards with BFS in Tree Structures
ladders.desc: Understand how to use BFS to explore multiple branches when encountering wildcard characters.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the central technique: using BFS to manage uncertainty from wildcards in a tree-like data structure. The intuition is that when searching a pattern with '.', we don't know which character to follow, so we must consider all possibilities. BFS allows us to keep track of all current possible nodes in the Trie at each position. By processing level by level (where each level corresponds to a character position), we can efficiently prune impossible paths. The algorithm: Maintain a queue of nodes. For each character in the search pattern: if it's a specific letter, filter the queue to nodes that have that child; if it's a wildcard, expand the queue to include all children of current nodes. This ensures we explore all viable paths without recursion. Time complexity can explode with many wildcards (O(26^d)), but BFS manages memory by only storing current frontier. Space complexity is proportional to the number of nodes at the current depth. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the BFS search subroutine. Common mistakes: Using DFS instead, which can be deeper recursion and harder to debug; or not resetting the queue properly between characters. Real code pattern: queue = [root]; for ch in word: next_queue = []; for node in queue: if ch == '.': next_queue.extend([child for child in node.children if child]); else: idx = ord(ch)-97; if node.children[idx]: next_queue.append(node.children[idx]); queue = next_queue;.

ladders.title: Building Block — Trie Data Structure for String Storage
ladders.desc: Implement a Trie (prefix tree) to store a set of words efficiently.
ladders.explanation: A Trie is a tree-like data structure used to store strings, where each node represents a character, and paths from root to nodes spell out words. It enables efficient prefix searches and insertions. The intuition is to share common prefixes among words, saving space and time. Each TrieNode typically has an array of children (size 26 for lowercase English letters) and a boolean flag is_end to mark the end of a word. Insertion: Start at root, for each character in the word, navigate to the corresponding child (create if missing), and mark the last node as end. Search (without wildcards): Traverse the Trie following characters; if at any point a child is missing, return false; after traversal, check is_end. Time complexity for insert and search: O(L) where L is word length. Space: O(N*L) in worst case, but often less due to prefix sharing. Connection to L2: The Trie provides the tree structure on which BFS operates for wildcard search. Common mistakes: Not initializing children array properly, forgetting to set is_end, or mishandling non-lowercase letters. Real code pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = [None]*26; self.is_end = False; class Trie: def __init__(self): self.root = TrieNode(); def insert(self, word): node = self.root; for ch in word: idx = ord(ch)-97; if not node.children[idx]: node.children[idx] = TrieNode(); node = node.children[idx]; node.is_end = True; def search(self, word): node = self.root; for ch in word: idx = ord(ch)-97; if not node.children[idx]: return False; node = node.children[idx]; return node.is_end.

Search 'ca': follow c->a, check a.is_end=false (since 'ca' not inserted as word), return false. Search 'dog': root.children[3] for 'd' is null, return false.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — BFS Traversal in a Tree
ladders.desc: Implement breadth-first search (BFS) to traverse all nodes level by level in a tree.
ladders.explanation: BFS is a graph traversal algorithm that explores nodes level by level, starting from a root node. It uses a queue data structure to keep track of nodes to visit. The intuition is to visit all neighbors at the current depth before moving deeper, which is useful for shortest path problems or level-order processing. Algorithm: 1. Initialize a queue with the root node. 2. While queue is not empty: a. Dequeue the front node. b. Process the node (e.g., print value). c. Enqueue all its children (or neighbors). This ensures nodes are visited in order of increasing depth. Time complexity: O(N) where N is number of nodes, as each node is enqueued and dequeued once. Space complexity: O(W) where W is the maximum width of the tree (size of queue). Connection to L3: BFS is used to traverse the Trie during wildcard search. Common mistakes: Using a stack instead of queue (which would be DFS), not marking visited nodes in graphs (but trees have no cycles), or infinite loops if children are re-enqueued incorrectly. Real code pattern: from collections import deque; def bfs(root): if not root: return; queue = deque([root]); while queue: node = queue.popleft(); print(node.val); for child in node.children: if child: queue.append(child).

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Trie Data Structure Basics
ladders.desc: Understand the Trie data structure: a tree used to store strings for efficient prefix searches.
ladders.explanation: A queue is a linear data structure that follows the First-In-First-Out (FIFO) principle. Elements are added at the rear (enqueue) and removed from the front (dequeue). It is used in scenarios where order needs to be preserved, such as BFS traversal, task scheduling, or buffering. The intuition is like a real-world queue: the first person to join is the first to be served. Basic operations: enqueue(item): add item to the rear; dequeue(): remove and return the front item; peek(): view front item without removing; is_empty(): check if queue is empty. Implementation can use arrays (with circular buffer to avoid shifting) or linked lists. Time complexity: O(1) for enqueue and dequeue if implemented properly. Space complexity: O(n) for storing elements. Connection to L4: BFS uses a queue to manage nodes to visit. Common mistakes: Using list append and pop(0) in Python which is O(n) for pop(0); instead use collections.deque for O(1) operations. Real code pattern: from collections import deque; queue = deque(); queue.append('a') # enqueue; item = queue.popleft() # dequeue; if not queue: # is_empty.

approaches.name: Trie with Caching O(m)
approaches.summary: Build a Trie and cache results of previous searches to avoid redundant computations for repeated queries with wildcards. This optimizes performance in scenarios with many similar searches by storing intermediate results.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(m) per query after caching, where m is word length
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(t + c) where t is Trie size and c is cache size

ladders.title: Full Problem — Word Dictionary with Wildcard Search using Trie and Caching
ladders.desc: Implement a word dictionary supporting insertion and search with '.' wildcard using Trie with caching for repeated queries.
ladders.explanation: This solution builds a Trie data structure to store words efficiently. Each node contains children (mapping characters to child nodes) and an end-of-word flag. For search operations, we handle '.' wildcards by exploring all possible child branches when encountering '.'. To optimize repeated searches, we implement a cache (dictionary) that stores results of previous search queries. When a search is requested, we first check the cache; if found, return the cached result immediately. Otherwise, perform a DFS traversal of the Trie, handling wildcards by recursively exploring all children at wildcard positions. Cache the result before returning. Intuition: The Trie allows prefix-based search, while caching eliminates redundant computations for identical or similar queries, crucial when the same wildcard pattern is searched multiple times. Step-by-step: 1. Define TrieNode class with children dict and is_end boolean. 2. In WordDictionary class, initialize root TrieNode and cache dict. 3. For addWord(word): traverse Trie from root, creating nodes as needed, mark end at last character. 4. For search(word): check cache for word; if present, return cached result. Otherwise, call recursive helper dfs(node, index) starting at root and index 0. In dfs: if index equals word length, return node.is_end. If word[index] is '.', iterate through all children of current node, recursively call dfs on each child with index+1; return true if any path returns true. Else, check if word[index] exists in node.children; if yes, recursively call dfs on that child with index+1; else return false. Cache the result (true/false) for word before returning. Time: O(m) per query after caching, where m is word length (worst-case O(26^m) without caching, but caching reduces repeated work). Space: O(t + c) where t is Trie size (nodes) and c is cache size. Connection to L1: This uses the recursive search helper as a key subroutine. Common mistakes: Not resetting cache when new words are added (cache should be invalidated or use versioning), forgetting to handle empty string searches. Real code pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = {}; self.is_end = False. class WordDictionary: def __init__(self): self.root = TrieNode(); self.cache = {}. def addWord(self, word): node = self.root; for ch in word: if ch not in node.children: node.children[ch] = TrieNode(); node = node.children[ch]; node.is_end = True. def search(self, word): if word in self.cache: return self.cache[word]; result = self.dfs(self.root, word, 0); self.cache[word] = result; return result. def dfs(self, node, word, i): if i == len(word): return node.is_end; ch = word[i]; if ch == '.': for child in node.children.values(): if self.dfs(child, word, i+1): return True; return False; else: if ch in node.children: return self.dfs(node.children[ch], word, i+1); return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive DFS Search with Wildcard Handling in Trie
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive depth-first search function that traverses the Trie to find if a word exists, handling '.' wildcards.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core search logic within the full problem. It performs a DFS on the Trie starting from a given node and index in the search word. The intuition is to recursively match characters: if we reach the end of the word, check if the current node marks the end of a word. For wildcard '.', we explore all possible child branches, returning true if any path succeeds. For regular characters, we follow the corresponding child if it exists. This approach leverages the Trie's structure to efficiently handle prefixes and wildcards. Step-by-step: Define function dfs(node, word, i): if i equals word length, return node.is_end. Let ch = word[i]. If ch is '.': for each child in node.children: if dfs(child, word, i+1) returns true, return true. Return false. Else: if ch exists in node.children, return dfs(node.children[ch], word, i+1). Else return false. Time: O(26^m) in worst-case without caching (due to wildcards exploring all branches), but typically O(m) for non-wildcard searches. Space: O(m) for recursion stack. Connection to L0: This function is called by the search method after cache check. Common mistakes: Not handling empty word correctly (i == len(word) should check node.is_end), infinite recursion if not incrementing i. Real code pattern: def dfs(self, node, word, i): if i == len(word): return node.is_end; ch = word[i]; if ch == '.': for child in node.children.values(): if self.dfs(child, word, i+1): return True; return False; else: if ch in node.children: return self.dfs(node.children[ch], word, i+1); return False.

examples.trace: Start: node=root, i=0, word='.ad', len=3. ch='.' (wildcard). Iterate children of root: b, d, m. For child b: dfs(b-node, '.ad', 1): ch='a' (since word[1]='a'), 'a' in b-node.children? Yes (b->a). dfs(a-node, '.ad', 2): ch='d', 'd' in a-node.children? Yes (a->d). dfs(d-node, '.ad', 3): i=3=len, d.is_end=True (since 'bad' ends here), return true. So early return true from root level.

Start: node=root, i=0, word='a.', len=2. ch='a' (not wildcard). 'a' in root.children? Yes. dfs(a-node, 'a.', 1): ch='.' (wildcard). Iterate children of a-node: only 'b'. For child b: dfs(b-node, 'a.', 2): i=2=len, b.is_end=True (since 'ab' ends here), return true. So return true.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Handling Wildcard '.' in Trie Traversal
ladders.desc: Implement the logic to traverse all possible paths when encountering a '.' wildcard during Trie search.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the central technique: when searching a word in a Trie and hitting a '.' wildcard character, we must explore all child branches of the current node. The intuition is that '.' can match any character, so we need to check every possible continuation in the Trie. This is done by iterating through all children of the current node and recursively searching from each child with the next character index. If any path leads to a successful match (i.e., finds a word end at the correct length), we return true immediately. This approach ensures we cover all possibilities without missing matches. Step-by-step: In a search function, at index i with character ch: if ch == '.': for each key in node.children: recursively call search on node.children[key] with index i+1. If any returns true, return true. Else return false. Time: O(b) where b is number of children at that node (up to 26 for lowercase letters), multiplied by recursive calls. Space: O(1) for iteration, plus recursion stack. Connection to L1: This is a part of the dfs subroutine. Common mistakes: Forgetting to return false if no child path succeeds, not handling nodes with no children (empty iteration). Real code pattern: if ch == '.': for child in node.children.values(): if search(child, word, i+1): return True; return False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Trie Node Structure and Basic Traversal
ladders.desc: Define the Trie node structure and implement basic traversal to check for a word without wildcards.
ladders.explanation: This level involves creating the Trie node and performing a simple search for a word without any wildcards. The Trie node typically contains a dictionary mapping characters to child nodes and a boolean flag indicating if the node represents the end of a word. The intuition is to store words in a tree where each path from the root corresponds to a prefix, allowing efficient prefix-based searches. For basic traversal, we start at the root and follow the characters of the word one by one, moving to the corresponding child node. If at any point the character is not found, the word does not exist. If we reach the end of the word, we check the end-of-word flag to confirm if it's a complete word. Step-by-step: Define class TrieNode with attributes: children (dict) and is_end (bool). For search(word): set node = root. For each character ch in word: if ch not in node.children, return false. Else, node = node.children[ch]. After loop, return node.is_end. Time: O(m) where m is word length. Space: O(1) extra space. Connection to L2: This is the foundation for handling non-wildcard characters in search. Common mistakes: Not initializing children as empty dict, forgetting to set is_end to True when adding words. Real code pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = {}; self.is_end = False. def search(word): node = self.root; for ch in word: if ch not in node.children: return False; node = node.children[ch]; return node.is_end.

examples.trace: Start at root. ch='c': 'c' in root.children? Yes, move to c-node. ch='a': 'a' in c-node.children? Yes, move to a-node. ch='t': 't' in a-node.children? Yes, move to t-node. End of word, check t-node.is_end: True (since 'cat' was added), return true.
Start at root. ch='d': 'd' in root.children? Yes, move to d-node. ch='o': 'o' in d-node.children? Yes, move to o-node. ch='t': 't' in o-node.children? No, return false.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Inserting a Word into a Trie
ladders.desc: Implement the function to add a word to the Trie by creating nodes as needed.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental operation of inserting a word into a Trie. The intuition is to build the Trie incrementally: for each character in the word, we traverse from the root, creating new nodes only when a character is not already present in the current node's children. This ensures that common prefixes are shared among words, saving space. After processing all characters, we mark the last node as an end-of-word node. Step-by-step: Start at the root node. For each character ch in the word: if ch is not in the current node's children dictionary, create a new TrieNode and add it to children under key ch. Move to that child node. After the loop, set the current node's is_end flag to True. Time: O(m) where m is word length, as we process each character once. Space: O(m) in worst-case if no prefixes shared, but typically less due to sharing. Connection to L3: This builds the Trie structure used for search. Common mistakes: Not creating nodes for all characters, forgetting to mark the end node. Real code pattern: def addWord(self, word): node = self.root; for ch in word: if ch not in node.children: node.children[ch] = TrieNode(); node = node.children[ch]; node.is_end = True.

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Trie Data Structure Basics
ladders.desc: Understand the Trie data structure: a tree used to store strings for efficient prefix searches.
ladders.explanation: A Trie (pronounced 'try' or derived from 'retrieval') is a tree-like data structure used to store a dynamic set of strings. It is particularly useful for tasks involving prefixes, such as autocomplete or dictionary lookups. The intuition is that each node in the Trie represents a character, and paths from the root to nodes spell out strings. This allows sharing of common prefixes among strings, making it memory-efficient and fast for prefix-based operations. Key concepts: 1. Root node: The starting point, typically empty. 2. Child nodes: Each node has children corresponding to possible next characters. 3. End-of-word marker: A flag at nodes indicating that a complete word ends there. For example, storing 'cat' and 'car' would share nodes for 'c' and 'a', then branch to 't' and 'r'. This structure enables operations like insertion, search, and prefix checking in O(m) time where m is string length. Step-by-step for basic understanding: To insert a word, start at root, for each character, go to or create child node, mark last node as end. To search a word, follow characters from root; if path exists and last node is marked end, word is found. Time: O(m) for both operations. Space: O(t) where t is total nodes, which can be up to total characters but often less due to sharing. Connection to L4: This is the underlying structure for inserting words. Common mistakes: Confusing Trie with other trees like binary search trees, not realizing that nodes can have multiple children. Real code pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = {}  # dictionary mapping char to TrieNode; self.is_end = False.

examples.output: Trie structure: root -> a -> n (is_end true for 'an') -> t (is_end true for 'ant') and -> d (is_end true for 'and').
